source_paper_title: EFX: A Simpler Approach and an (Almost) Optimal Guarantee via Rainbow Cycle Number
title: Determine existence of complete EFX allocations for three agents with monotone valuations
background_latex:
Let [1mDiscrete fair division[0m consist of a finite set of indivisible goods \(M\) with \(|M|=m\) and \(n\) agents \([n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\). Each agent \(i\) has a valuation function \(v_i:2^M\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\) that is [1mnormalized[0m \(v_i(\varnothing)=0\) and [1mmonotone[0m: for all \(S\subseteq M\) and \(g\in M\setminus S\), \(v_i(S\cup\{g\})\ge v_i(S)\).

An [1mallocation[0m is a partition \(X=\langle X_1,\dots,X_n\rangle\) of \(M\) into disjoint bundles whose union is \(M\); such an allocation is [1mcomplete[0m because every good is assigned to some agent.

For two agents \(i,i'\), agent \(i\) has [1mEFX-envy[0m toward \(i'\) if there exists a good \(g\in X_{i'}\) such that
\[
 v_i(X_i) < v_i(X_{i'}\setminus\{g\}).
\]
An allocation \(X\) is [1menvy-free up to any good (EFX)[0m if no agent has EFX-envy toward any other agent, equivalently, for all \(i,i'\in[n]\) and all \(g\in X_{i'}\),
\[
 v_i(X_i) \ge v_i(X_{i'}\setminus\{g\}).
\]
The general existence of complete EFX allocations for monotone valuations is a central question in discrete fair division; even for \(n=3\) it is not settled for unrestricted monotone valuations.
problem_definition_latex:
For \(n=3\) agents with normalized monotone valuation functions \(v_1,v_2,v_3:2^M\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\) over a finite set of indivisible goods \(M\), determine whether there always exists a complete allocation \(X=\langle X_1,X_2,X_3\rangle\) (a partition of \(M\)) that is EFX, i.e., satisfies
\[
\forall i,i'\in\{1,2,3\},\ \forall g\in X_{i'}:\quad v_i(X_i) \ge v_i(X_{i'}\setminus\{g\}).
\]
Equivalently: either prove existence for all such instances or provide a counterexample instance (monotone valuations and goods set) with no complete EFX allocation.

For \(n=3\) agents with normalized monotone valuation functions \(v_1,v_2,v_3:2^M\to\mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\) over a finite set of indivisible goods \(M\), determine whether there always exists a complete allocation \(X=\langle X_1,X_2,X_3\rangle\) (a partition of \(M\)) that is EFX, i.e., satisfies
\[
\forall i,i'\in\{1,2,3\},\ \forall g\in X_{i'}:\quad v_i(X_i) \ge v_i(X_{i'}\setminus\{g\}).
\]
Equivalently: either prove existence for all such instances or provide a counterexample instance (monotone valuations and goods set) with no complete EFX allocation.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: A counterexample (if it exists) would likely be representable with a small number of goods and succinct valuations, making automated search plausible. However, the space of general monotone set functions is enormous, so one would need to restrict to a parameterized family (e.g., small m with explicit value tables), and verifying nonexistence of any EFX allocation requires checking all partitions, which grows quickly with m.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can encode candidate hard instances via explicit value tables for small numbers of goods and use a fitness signal based on the minimum EFX-violation over all allocations (computed by exhaustive enumeration). Evolutionary search could plausibly discover candidate counterexamples or suggest structural patterns, but scaling beyond very small m is difficult and proving impossibility for all m would still require theory.

verification.notice: Extracted from the source paper and passed an automated fidelity audit; not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis: No relevant Scholar-organic forward citations were available for this problem. The forward-citation review therefore cannot identify follow-up work that resolves or materially advances the problem.
